serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of eggplant tempura is one to two pieces, each approximately 50 g, representing a single battered and deep-fried eggplant slice as served in a Japanese restaurant or bento set.

nutrient_source.title: Eggplant Tempura — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all core macros and secondary nutrients. Values preserved as-is from the HPB Foodo database entry for eggplant tempura (F14080012). Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tempura-battered fried vegetables (NDB 35193) as an approximate supplement and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for eggplant tempura exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the GI of tempura batter (refined wheat flour, GI ~65–70 per Atkinson et al. 2008 for white wheat flour products) as the dominant glycaemic driver. Eggplant itself has a negligible GI contribution. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (23.34 g × 0.50 kg serving) / 100 = 65 × 11.67 / 100 ≈ 7.6 per 50 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (61 mg/100 g); no added high-sodium condiments in base preparation
hypertension.guidance: Eggplant tempura contains only 61 mg sodium per 100 g, well below the 200 mg/100 g threshold of concern; however, patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit tempura dipping sauce (tentsuyu), which is high in sodium, and be mindful that the high fat content (24.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying contributes to caloric density and may indirectly affect cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour tempura batter with medium-to-high GI (~65) and 23.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 2.4 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined wheat flour batter in eggplant tempura has an estimated GI of 65 and delivers 23.3 g of carbohydrate per 100 g with limited fibre buffering (2.4 g), promoting post-prandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with high-fibre foods and lean protein, and prefer grilled or steamed eggplant preparations to minimise glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (24.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying, including estimated saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g) and potential trans fat from repeated oil use
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process adds substantial total fat (24.2 g/100 g), with an estimated saturated fat contribution of approximately 3.2 g/100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of deep-fried tempura preparations and opt for eggplant cooked by grilling, steaming, or stir-frying with minimal oil to reduce saturated fat and caloric load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end products (AGEs); eggplant is a nightshade vegetable with potential sensitivity in some RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Eggplant tempura is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation, and eggplant belongs to the nightshade family (Solanaceae), which some RA patients report worsens symptoms; avoidance of this preparation is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Eggplant tempura (ナスの天ぷら, nasu no tempura) is a classic Japanese dish consisting of sliced eggplant (aubergine) coated in a light, airy tempura batter made from wheat flour, egg, and cold water, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crisp. The result is a dish with a delicate, slightly crispy exterior and a soft, creamy interior, as eggplant becomes tender and absorbs some of the frying oil during cooking. Eggplant tempura is commonly served as part of a tempura platter (tempura moriawase), as a side dish in a Japanese set meal (teishoku), or as a topping for tempura udon or soba. It is typically accompanied by tentsuyu dipping sauce (a blend of dashi, mirin, and soy sauce) and grated daikon radish. Nutritionally, eggplant tempura is energy-dense at approximately 323 kcal per 100 g, driven by the high fat content of 24.2 g/100 g from deep-frying. Carbohydrates contribute 23.3 g/100 g, primarily from the refined wheat flour batter, with a modest dietary fibre content of 2.4 g/100 g. Protein is low at 3.1 g/100 g. Sodium is relatively low at 61 mg/100 g in the base preparation, though dipping sauces significantly increase sodium intake. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 65 (medium), driven by the refined flour batter. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to the medium-GI refined batter and high fat load, and for those with cardiovascular disease due to the deep-frying fat content. It is also unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to AGE formation during frying and the nightshade classification of eggplant. It is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans due to egg in the batter.
common_user_queries: How many calories in eggplant tempura?
Is eggplant tempura high in fat?
Can I eat eggplant tempura with hypertension?
What is the difference between eggplant tempura and grilled eggplant?
meal_context: Japanese restaurant appetiser or side dish

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database at confidence 0.95 for the exact food item F14080012. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy tempura-battered fried vegetable analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous wheat flour product data with full documentation. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for a deep-fried battered vegetable dish.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) is an approximate estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for tempura-battered fried vegetables (closest approved-source analogue), as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be updated when HPB publishes a full fatty acid profile for F14080012. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific eggplant tempura preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. Glycemic index of 65 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat flour products from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for eggplant tempura exists in any approved GI source. 4. Eggplant is a nightshade vegetable (Solanaceae family); this is noted in the rheumatoid arthritis health context. 5. The vegan flag is set to false because traditional tempura batter contains egg; a vegan tempura variant using aquafaba or sparkling water exists but is not the canonical preparation.
